paper.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

executive_summary: The paper is well grounded in neural retrieval and sequence-to-sequence literature, but several claims about factuality and knowledge freshness need narrower wording. Evidentia found strong support for the core retrieval-augmented architecture, moderate support for downstream generalization claims, and a methods gap around evaluation of hallucination reduction.

citations.title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
citations.superseded_notes: Still foundational, but later retrieval-augmented and tool-using systems provide stronger evidence for current production settings.

superseded_by.title: Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models

citations.title: RAG Systems Eliminate Hallucinations in Enterprise Search
citations.superseded_notes: Non-peer-reviewed and overstates hallucination reduction; not appropriate as primary evidence.

claims.text: Retrieval augmentation improves factual accuracy on knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.

counterarguments.summary: Later work shows retrieval can introduce irrelevant or conflicting evidence when retrievers are poorly calibrated.

papers.title: When Not to Trust Language Models: Investigating Effectiveness of Parametric and Non-Parametric Memories
papers.relevance: Shows that retrieval quality and memory choice affect factual reliability.

supporting_sources.title: Atlas: Few-shot Learning with Retrieval Augmented Language Models
supporting_sources.note: Supports retrieval benefits in few-shot settings.

claims.text: The proposed system eliminates hallucinations by grounding every answer in retrieved passages.

counterarguments.summary: RAG can reduce unsupported generations, but hallucinations persist when retrieval is incomplete, stale, or contradicts the generation objective.

papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.relevance: Surveys remaining failure modes in RAG pipelines.

claims.text: Updating the retrieval index is cheaper than retraining the full parametric model.

supporting_sources.title: REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
supporting_sources.note: Supports the separation between retrieved memory and parametric model updates.

data_quality.summary: The evaluation reports downstream task gains but does not isolate retrieval errors from generation errors. Hallucination reduction is discussed qualitatively without confidence intervals or adversarial retrieval tests.

comparisons.field_norm: Multiple retriever variants and retrieval-noise tests

final_verdict.status: Needs to improve methods & data processes
final_verdict.summary: The sourcing is strong enough to support the architecture, but the methods section needs stronger evaluation before the factuality claims are submission-ready.
rationale: Source quality is strong overall, but one hallucination claim relies on a non-peer-reviewed source.
Coverage is uneven: the architecture claim is supported, while the hallucination-elimination claim is not.
Data quality is limited by missing factuality metrics, confidence intervals, and retrieval-noise ablations.
next_steps: Replace the product blog with peer-reviewed RAG evaluation literature.
Narrow the hallucination claim from elimination to reduction under tested retrieval conditions.
Add retrieval ablations, factuality metrics, and error analysis separating retriever and generator failures.
